blocks:
Ayahs

لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ (١)
I swear by this town, (1)
وَأَنتَ حِلٌّۢ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ (٢)
as you reside in this town; (2)
وَوَالِدٍ وَمَا وَلَدَ (٣)
[and] by the father and him whom he begot: (3)
لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِى كَبَدٍ (٤)
certainly We created man in travail. (4)
أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّن يَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ أَحَدٌ (٥)
Does he suppose that no one will ever have power over him? (5)
يَقُولُ أَهْلَكْتُ مَالًا لُّبَدًا (٦)
He says, ‘I have squandered immense wealth.’ (6)
أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّمْ يَرَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ (٧)
Does he suppose that no one sees him? (7)

Content and Virtue of Surah al-Balad

Despite its brevity, this Surah contains profound truths.

  1. In the first part of this Surah, after mentioning meaningful oaths, reference is made to the fact that human life in this world is always coupled with difficulties and toil. This is so that, on one hand, man may prepare himself to battle hardships, and on the other, he may dismiss the expectation of absolute peace and comfort in this world; absolute peace is possible only in the life of the Hereafter and nowhere else.

  2. In another section of this Surah, some of the most important Divine blessings upon man are enumerated, followed by a reference to his ingratitude toward these blessings.

  3. In the final section of this Surah, people are divided into two groups: "the Companions of the Right" (Ashab al-Maymanah) and "the Companions of the Left" (Ashab al-Mash'amah). It describes some of the qualities and deeds of the first group (the righteous believers) and their destiny, and then addresses their opposite—the disbelievers and criminals—and their fate.

The expression of the verses is very decisive and striking, the phrasing is short and resonant, and the words are highly effective and eloquent. The form and content of the verses indicate that this Surah is among the "Meccan" Surahs.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Balad, Allah will grant him safety from His wrath on the Day of Resurrection." ^1

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 490.

And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites the Surah 'I swear by this city' (La uqsimu bihadha al-balad) in his obligatory prayers will be known among the righteous in this world, and in the Hereafter, he will be recognized as one who holds a rank and status before Allah, and he will be among the companions of the Prophets, the martyrs, and the righteous." ^1

^1: Thawab al-A'mal as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 578.

In the Name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful

I swear by this city (1) And you are an inhabitant of this city (2) And [by] the father and that which he fathered (3) We have certainly created man in exertion (4)

Does he think that never will anyone have power over him? (5) He says, "I have squandered wealth in abundance" (6) Does he think that no one has seen him? (7)

Commentary

An Oath by This Holy City

The tradition of the Quran in many instances is to begin the statement of very important truths with oaths—oaths that themselves stimulate human thought, reflection, and intellect; oaths that have a specific connection to the subject at hand.

Here too, to state the reality that human life in the world is coupled with pain and toil, it begins with a fresh oath and says: "I swear by this holy city, the city of Mecca" (لا أُقْسِمُ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ) ^1

"The city in which you reside" (وَ أَنْتَ حِلٌّ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ). Although the name "Mecca" is not explicitly mentioned in these verses, given that the Surah is Meccan on one hand, and considering the extraordinary importance of this holy city on the other, it is clear that Mecca is intended, and the consensus of commentators is the same.

Of course, the nobility and greatness of the land of Mecca necessitate that Allah

^1: "La" here is "extra" (za'idah) and serves for emphasis; however, according to another interpretation, it is possible that "La" is negatory (further explanation on this was provided at the beginning of Surah al-Qiyamah).

swears by it, for the first center of monotheism and worship of the Lord was built here, and great Prophets have circumambulated this House. However, the phrase "And you are an inhabitant of this city" contains a new point: it says that this city, because of your grace-filled and blessed presence, has attained such greatness that it has become worthy of this oath.

And the truth is that the value of lands depends on the value of the people residing in them. Let the disbelievers of Mecca not imagine that if the Quran swears by this land, it is because it values their homeland or the center of their idols; no, it is not so. The only value of this city (aside from its specific historical background) is due to the existence of the noble servant of God, Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله.

O you, by the blessing of whose arrival the Kaaba gained a hundred honors, And the dead, from your holy arrival, gained a hundred purities. Batha (Mecca) has found radiance from the light of your countenance, Yathrib (Medina) from your dust has gained splendor and melody.

There is also another interpretation here: "I do not swear by this holy city while they have violated your sanctity, and deemed your life, property, and honor permissible (halal)."

This is a severe rebuke and reproach toward the disbelievers of the Quraish, who considered themselves the servants and guardians of the Sanctuary of Mecca. They held such respect for this land that even if their father's killer was seen there, he was safe; it is even said that those who took bark from the trees of Mecca and wore it were safe because of it. Yet, why were all these customs and traditions trampled upon regarding the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله?! Why did they permit every kind of harassment against him and his companions, and even deem their blood permissible?!

This interpretation has also been narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. ^1

Then He adds: "And [by] the father and that which he fathered" (وَ والِدٍ وَ ما وَلَدَ). Regarding who is meant by this "father" and "child," several interpretations have been mentioned:

First: The "father" is Ibrahim, the Friend of Allah (Khalil), and the "child" is Ismail, the sacrificed (Dhabih). Given that the previous verse swore by the city of "Mecca," and we know that Ibrahim and his son were the founders of the Kaaba and the city of Mecca, this interpretation seems very appropriate, especially since the pre-Islamic Arabs also held Prophet Ibrahim and his son in extraordinary regard, took pride in them, and many traced their lineage back to them.

Second: It refers to Adam and his children.

Third: It refers to Adam and the Prophets who rose from his progeny.

Fourth: It refers to an oath by every father and child, for the matter of birth and the survival of the human race throughout various eras is among the most amazing wonders of creation, and Allah has specifically sworn by it.

Combining these four interpretations is also not unlikely, though the first interpretation seems the most fitting of all.

Then He addresses that which is the ultimate goal of these oaths, saying: "We have certainly created man in exertion" (لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي كَبَدٍ).

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 493.

"Kabad," according to Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, originally means "intensity" or "hardness," which is why when milk becomes thick, they say "takabbada al-laban."

However, according to Raghib in al-Mufradat, "kabad" (rhyming with hasad) refers to a pain that afflicts a person's "kabd" (liver), and subsequently, it was applied to every kind of hardship and toil.

Whatever the root of this word may be, its current meaning is toil and discomfort.

Yes, man, from the beginning of life—even from the moment his zygote is placed in the womb—undergoes many stages of difficulties, pain, and toil until he is born. After birth, during childhood, then youth, and most difficult of all, the period of old age, he faces various hardships and toils. This is the nature of worldly life, and to expect otherwise is a mistake. As the Arabic poet says:

It was fashioned upon gloom, yet you desire it To be pure from glooms and impurities? He who tasks the days against their nature Is like one seeking a spark of fire within water!

A look at the lives of the Prophets and the Friends of Allah (Awliya) also shows that the lives of these finest flowers of creation were also coupled with various unpleasantries, pains, and toils. When the world is such for them, the situation for others is clear.

And if we see individuals or societies that seemingly have no pain or toil, it is either due to our superficial observations—whereby if we look closer, we become acquainted with the depth of the pains and toils of

these very owners of a luxurious life—or it is for a limited and exceptional time that does not break the general law of the world.

Then He adds: "Does this man think that never will anyone have power over him?" (أَ يَحْسَبُ أَنْ لَنْ يَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ أَحَدٌ). ^1 This points to the fact that the entanglement of human life with all that pain and toil is proof that he has no absolute power.

Yet he is mounted upon the steed of arrogance, committing every wrong, sin, crime, and transgression as if he sees himself in safety and security, imagining himself beyond the realm of Divine punishment. When he reaches power, he tramples all Divine laws and is absolutely not a servant to God. Does he truly think he will escape the grasp of the Lord's punishment? What a great mistake!

It has also been suggested that this refers to the wealthy who thought no one had the power to take their wealth.

And it has also been said it refers to those who believed no one would call them to account for their actions.

However, the verse has a comprehensive meaning that can include all these interpretations.

Some have said: The above verse refers to a man from the tribe of Jumah named "Abu al-Asad." He was so strong that he would sit on a piece of leather, and ten men would try to pull it from under his feet but could not; the leather would tear into pieces while he

would not budge! ^1 However, the verse's reference to such an individual or arrogant individuals does not prevent the generality of its meaning.

Then, continuing the same discourse, He adds: "He says, 'I have squandered wealth in abundance'" (يَقُولُ أَهْلَكْتُ مالًا لُبَداً). This refers to those who, when suggested to spend wealth on a good cause, would say out of pride and haughtiness: "We have spent much in these ways," while they had spent nothing for the sake of God. If they had given wealth to this or that person, it was for showing off, hypocrisy, and personal motives.

Some have also said: The verse refers to those who spent vast amounts of wealth in enmity against Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and in anti-Islamic conspiracies, and took pride in it. As mentioned in a hadith, on the day of the Battle of the Trench, when Ali عليه السلام offered "Islam" to "Amr ibn Abd Wudd," he replied:

"Then what becomes of all that wealth I spent in abundance against you?!" [^2]

Some have also said: The verse pertains to some leaders of the Quraish, such as "Harith ibn 'Amir," who had committed a sin and asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for the way of salvation. The Prophet ordered him to pay an expiation (kaffarah). He said: "My wealth has been destroyed in expiations and expenditures since the day I entered the religion of Islam!" [^3]

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 493.

[^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 580, Hadith 10.

[^3]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 493.

Combining all three interpretations is also possible, though the first interpretation is more consistent with the following verse.

The expression "ahalktu" (I have destroyed/squandered) indicates that his wealth has, in reality, been wasted and no benefit will return to him.

"Lubad" (rhyming with lughat) means something accumulated and dense, and here it means abundant wealth.

Then He adds: "Does he think that no one has seen him [and does not see him]?" (أَ يَحْسَبُ أَنْ لَمْ يَرَهُ أَحَدٌ). He is heedless of the fact that Allah not only sees the outward appearance of his deeds in private and public but is also aware of the depths of his heart and soul, and is informed of his intentions. Is it possible for a God whose infinite existence encompasses everything not to see or know something?! These heedless ones, due to their ignorance and lack of awareness, imagine themselves beyond the constant surveillance of the Lord.

Yes, Allah knows from where he obtained this wealth and in what way he spent it! In a hadith narrated from Ibn Abbas, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"The feet of a servant shall not move [on the Day of Resurrection] until he is asked about four things: about his life, how he spent it; about his wealth, from where he gathered it and on what he spent it; about his deeds, what he did; and about the love for us, the Ahl al-Bayt!" ^1

In short, how can man become arrogant and claim power when his life is kneaded with pain and toil? "If he has wealth, it is subject to a night's disaster, and if he has life, it is subject to a fever!" Furthermore, how can he claim that he has spent abundant wealth in the way of God, while He is aware of his intention? He knows both the quality of the acquisition of that illegitimate wealth and the manner of its hypocritical and biased expenditure.

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 494—a similar meaning is also found in the commentary Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 435.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عَيْنَيْنِ (٨)
Have We not made for him two eyes, (8)
وَلِسَانًا وَشَفَتَيْنِ (٩)
a tongue, and two lips, (9)
وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلنَّجْدَيْنِ (١٠)
and shown him the two paths [of good and evil]? (10)

Commentary

The Blessing of Sight, Speech, and Guidance

Following the previous verses which spoke of the arrogance and negligence of rebellious humans, the verses under discussion enumerate some of the most important material and spiritual blessings of Allah bestowed upon man. This is done, on one hand, to break his pride and heedlessness, and on the other, to compel him to reflect upon the Creator of these blessings, and by stirring the sense of gratitude within his soul, lead him toward the gnosis of the Creator.

First, He says: "Have We not made for him two eyes?" (أَ لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُ عَيْنَيْنِ).

"And a tongue and two lips?" (وَ لِساناً وَ شَفَتَيْنِ).

"And shown him the two ways [of good and evil]?" (وَ هَدَيْناهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ).

In this manner, within these few short sentences, He has pointed to three vital material blessings and one great spiritual blessing, all of which are among the most magnificent of divine favors: the blessing of the eyes, the tongue, and the lips on one side, and the blessing of guidance and the knowledge of good and evil on the other.

(Note that Najd originally means an elevated place, in contrast to Tihamah which refers to lowlands; or in other words, "highland" and "lowland." Here, it is a metaphor for good and evil, and the paths of felicity and wretchedness) [1]. Regarding the importance of the aforementioned blessings, it suffices to say:

The "eye" is the most important means of human connection with the outside world. The wonders of the eye are such that they truly compel man to humble himself before its Creator. The seven layers of the eye—named the sclera (cornea), choroid, iris, lens, aqueous humor, vitreous humor, and retina—each have a strange, delicate, and amazing structure in which the physical and chemical laws related to light and mirrors are observed in the most precise manner. So much so that the most advanced precision cameras are worthless objects in comparison. Truly, if there were nothing else in the entire world but man, and nothing else in man's entire existence but the eye, studying its wonders would be sufficient to recognize the vast knowledge and power of the Lord.

[1]: This interpretation is narrated in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام (Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed). The interpretation by some that it refers to the mother's two breasts (due to the protrusion of the chest) is very far-fetched. Furthermore, the use of the term Najd for "good" is due to its greatness, and for "evil," it is by way of association (taghlib).

As for the "tongue," it is the most important means of communication between humans and the transfer and exchange of information and knowledge from one people to another, and from one generation to the next. If it were not for this means of communication, man could never have progressed to this extent in science, material civilization, and spiritual matters.

As for the "lips," firstly, they play an effective role in speech, as many articulations of letters are performed by the lips. Beyond this, the lips greatly assist in chewing food, maintaining the moisture of the mouth, and drinking water. If they did not exist, the matter of human eating and drinking—and even the appearance of the face due to the outward flow of saliva and the inability to pronounce many letters—would be in a pitiable state.

And since the perception of realities is primarily through the eye and the tongue;

Following these, He points to the existence of "intellect" and "rational and innate guidance," and the expression of the verse even encompasses "legislative guidance" which is carried out through the Prophets and the Awliya (Saints).

Yes, He has placed both the "seeing eye" and the "sun" at man's disposal, and has shown him both the "path and the pit," so that "man may look before his own feet."

However, despite all these glowing lamps placed along his path, if he goes astray, it must be said: "Let him fall and see his own desert!" The phrase "وَ هَدَيْناهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ" (We showed him the two ways) besides expressing the issue of human choice and free will, and considering that Najd is an elevated place, indicates that traversing the path of good is not devoid of difficulties, toil, and hardship—just as climbing high ground has its difficulties. Even traversing the path of evil has its difficulties; how much better it is for man to choose the path of good through his effort and striving.

Yet, at the same time, the choice of the path rests with man himself. It is he who can direct the eye and the tongue toward the permissible or the forbidden, and choose whichever he wishes from the two roads of "good" and "evil."

Therefore, we read in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Allah the Almighty says to the children of Adam:

'O son of Adam! If your tongue disputes with you regarding what I have forbidden to you, I have aided you against it with two lips, so close them. And if your sight disputes with you toward some of what I have forbidden to you, I have aided you against it with two eyelids, so close them...'" [1] In this way, Allah has also placed the means of controlling these great blessings at man's disposal, and this is another of His great favors.

It is interesting that in the aforementioned verses, regarding the tongue, the lips are mentioned, but regarding the eyes, the eyelids are not mentioned. This is apparently for two reasons: one is that the role of the lips for speaking, eating, and other aspects is far greater than that of the eyelids for the eye; and the other is that controlling the tongue is far more important and decisive for one's destiny than controlling the eye.

Key Points

1- The Wonders of the Eye

The eye is usually compared to a camera that takes photographs of various scenes with its very delicate lens—

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 581.

photographs that are reflected on the "retina" instead of film, and from there are transmitted to the brain via the optic nerves.

This incredibly delicate and precise photographic apparatus may take thousands upon thousands of photos of different scenes in a day and night, yet in many respects, it is not comparable even to the most advanced photographic and filming equipment, because:

1- The light regulation aperture (diaphragm) in this device, which is the "pupil," automatically changes shape in response to light—becoming narrower in strong light and wider in weak light—whereas cameras must be adjusted by individuals.

2- The lens of the eye, unlike all lenses used in the world's cameras, constantly changes shape, such that its diameter is sometimes 1.5 mm and sometimes reaches up to 8 mm! This is to prepare for photographing distant and near scenes, and this task is performed by the muscles surrounding the lens that pull or release it, such that a single eye lens alone performs the work of hundreds of lenses!

3- This photographic device moves in four different directions and can move to any side to film with the help of the eye muscles.

4- Another important point is that in cameras, the films must be changed; when one roll of film is finished, another must be put in its place. But human eyes film throughout one's entire life without anything being replaced. This is because in the retina, where images are reflected, there are two types of cells: "cone" and "rod" cells, which contain a substance highly sensitive to light. With the slightest flash of light, it decomposes and creates waves that are transmitted to the brain, after which its effect vanishes, and the retina becomes ready for new filming again.

5- Cameras are made of very sturdy materials, but the photographic apparatus of the eye is so delicate and soft that it can be scratched by the slightest thing. For this reason, it is placed in a sturdy bony socket, yet despite its delicacy, it is more durable than iron and steel.

6- The issue of "light adjustment" is a very important matter for cinematographers and photographers; to ensure images are clear, it is sometimes necessary to spend several hours adjusting the light and its preliminaries. Meanwhile, the eye takes photos in all conditions—strong, medium, and weak light—even in darkness, provided there is a very small amount of light there, and this is among the wonders of the eye.

7- Sometimes we go from light into darkness, or the electric lamps are suddenly turned off; at that moment, we see absolutely nothing. But after a few moments, our eye automatically adapts itself to that weak light, such that when we look around, we gradually see many objects and say our eyes "got used" to the darkness. This expression "habit," uttered in simple language, is the result of a very complex mechanism within the eye that can adapt itself to new conditions in a very short time.

The reverse of this is also true when we move from darkness into light; initially, our eye cannot tolerate the strong light, but after a few moments, it adapts itself and, so to speak, gets used to it. These things never exist in filming equipment.

8- Filming devices can produce film from a limited space, whereas the human eye sees the entire semicircle of the horizon in front of it. In other words, we see nearly 180 degrees of the circle around us, while no camera is like this.

9- Among the strange and interesting facts is that the two human eyes, each being an independent device, are so synchronized that the images resulting from them fall exactly on one point, such that if this synchronization is slightly disturbed, man sees a single object as two with his two eyes! As is observed in "strabismic" (double-vision) individuals.

10- Another interesting point is that all scenes photographed by the eye fall upside down on the retina, yet we see nothing upside down. This is due to the eye's adaptation and the preservation of the ratio of objects to one another.

11- The surface of the eye must be constantly moist, such that if it dries for a few hours, it suffers a severe blow. This permanent moisture is provided by the tear glands, which enter the eye from one side and exit through very thin and delicate vessels located in the corners of the eyes, transferring to the nose and moistening the nose as well.

If the tear glands dry up, the eye is endangered and the movement of the eyelids becomes impossible. If they are overactive, tears constantly flow down the face; or if the narrow drainage path of the eye is blocked, one must constantly have a handkerchief in hand to dry the excess water from the face—and what a great trouble that is.

12- The composition of tears (eye water) is a complex one, consisting of more than ten elements, which together form the best and most suitable liquid for maintaining the eye.

In short, the wonders of the eye are so numerous that one must sit for days to speak about them and write books, yet when we look at its basic material, it is almost nothing more than a piece of fat! The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in his profound words:

"Marvel at this man: he sees with fat, speaks with flesh, hears with bone, and breathes through a slit!" (And he performs these great vital tasks with these small means) [1].

2- The Wonders of the Tongue

The tongue, in its turn, is one of the most amazing organs of the human body and carries heavy responsibilities. Besides providing effective help in swallowing food, it also plays an important role in chewing; it constantly pushes the morsel of food under the hammer of the teeth! Yet it performs this so skillfully that it keeps itself away from the strikes of the teeth, even though it is constantly beside and attached to them! Occasionally, we have rarely bitten our own tongue while chewing food, and our cry has risen, and we realized what would happen to us if that skill were not in the tongue.

Additionally, after eating, it sweeps and cleans the space of the mouth and teeth.

[1]: Nahj al-Balaghah, "Short Sayings," Wisdom 8.

And more important than these tasks is the matter of speaking, which is performed with rapid, organized, and successive movements, moving in six directions.

It is interesting that Allah has provided a means for speaking and talking to humans that is very easy, simple, and accessible to all; it brings neither fatigue nor boredom, nor does it have any cost.

Even more amazing than that is the talent for speech in man, which has been entrusted to the human soul, allowing man to construct many sentences in infinite different forms to express his incredibly diverse purposes.

And even more important than that is the talent for establishing various languages, the importance of which becomes clear by studying the thousands of languages existing in the world. Truly, "Greatness belongs to Allah, the One, the Subduer!"

3- Guidance to the "Two Ways" (Najdayn)

Najd, as we said, means an elevation or high land, and here it refers to the path of "good" and the path of "evil." In a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said:

"O people! There are two highlands: the highland of good and the highland of evil; and the highland of evil has not been made more beloved to you than the highland of good." [1] Undoubtedly, "duty" and responsibility are not possible without "recognition and awareness," and Allah, according to the above verse, has placed this awareness at the disposal of humans.

This awareness is achieved through three ways: through intellectual perceptions and reasoning; through nature (fitrah) and conscience without the need for reasoning; and through revelation and the teachings of the Prophets and their successors. Whatever man needs in traversing the path of perfection, Allah has taught him through one of these three ways, or in many cases, through all three.

It is noteworthy that in this Hadith, it is explicitly stated that traversing one of these two paths is not easier for human nature than the other. This, in fact, negates the general perception that man has a greater inclination toward evils and that traversing the path of evil is easier for him.

Truly, if there were no wrong upbringing and corrupt environments, man's love and affection for goodness would be very great. Perhaps the use of the term Najd (highland) regarding goodness is for this reason, as highlands have better and more pleasant air; and regarding evils, it is by way of association.

Some have also said that this expression refers to the prominence and clarity of the path of good and evil, just as a highland is completely visible [1].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 494, and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 7155. [1]: Just as the moon and the sun are called "the two moons" (qamaran).

Ayahs

فَلَا ٱقْتَحَمَ ٱلْعَقَبَةَ (١١)
Yet he has not embarked upon the uphill task. (11)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْعَقَبَةُ (١٢)
And what will show you what is the uphill task? (12)
فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ (١٣)
[It is] the freeing of a slave, (13)
أَوْ إِطْعَـٰمٌ فِى يَوْمٍ ذِى مَسْغَبَةٍ (١٤)
or feeding, during days of [general] starvation, (14)
يَتِيمًا ذَا مَقْرَبَةٍ (١٥)
an orphan among relatives (15)
أَوْ مِسْكِينًا ذَا مَتْرَبَةٍ (١٦)
or a needy man in desolation, (16)
ثُمَّ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلْمَرْحَمَةِ (١٧)
while being one of those who have faith and enjoin one another to patience, and enjoin one another to compassion. (17)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ (١٨)
They are the People of the Right Hand. (18)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ (١٩)
But those who defy Our signs, they are the People of the Left Hand. (19)
عَلَيْهِمْ نَارٌ مُّؤْصَدَةٌۢ (٢٠)
A closed Fire will be [imposed] upon them. (20)

Commentary

The Difficult Pass!

Following the mention of the great blessings in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion reproach and rebuke ungrateful servants for how, despite possessing all the means for prosperity, they have not traversed the path of salvation. First, It says:

"But he has not attempted the steep pass" (فَلَا اقْتَحَمَ الْعَقَبَةَ) [1]

[1]: It appears that "la" (لَا) in this sentence is "negative" and "declarative." The view of some who consider it a prayer for a curse or interrogative seems very unlikely. The only issue here is that when "la" precedes a past tense verb, it is usually repeated, as we read in Surah Al-Qiyamah, verse 31: "فَلَا صَدَّقَ وَلَا صَلَّى" (He neither believed nor prayed). However, it is not repeated in the verse under discussion. As the late Tabarsi noted in Majma' al-Bayan, it is sometimes used without repetition. Fakhr Razi and Qurtubi, in their commentaries, quote some masters of Arabic literature stating that if "la" carries the meaning of "lam" (did not), repetition is not necessary. There is also the possibility that the repetition is implied: "He neither attempted the steep pass, nor freed a slave, nor fed on a day of hunger."

Regarding what is meant by "the pass" ('aqabah) here, the subsequent verses explain it.

It asks: "And what will explain to you what the steep pass is?" (وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْعَقَبَةُ).

"It is the freeing of a slave" (فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ).

"Or feeding on a day of severe hunger" (أَوْ إِطْعَامٌ فِي يَوْمٍ ذِي مَسْغَبَةٍ).

"An orphan of near relationship" (يَتِيماً ذَا مَقْرَبَةٍ).

"Or a needy person in misery [lying in the dust]" (أَوْ مِسْكِيناً ذَا مَتْرَبَةٍ).

Thus, this difficult pass, which ungrateful humans have never prepared themselves to cross, is a collection of righteous deeds that primarily revolve around serving creation and helping the weak and powerless, as well as a set of correct and pure beliefs mentioned in the following verses.

Truly, crossing this pass is not an easy task, given the intense love most people have for wealth and property.

Islam and faith are not achieved through mere claims and words; rather, before every Muslim and believer lie difficult passes that they must cross one after another, by the power and strength of Allah, and by seeking aid from the spirit of faith and sincerity.

Some have interpreted "the pass" here as the "carnal soul" (hawa al-nafs), the struggle against which the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله termed the "Greater Jihad" (Jihad al-Akbar) according to a well-known hadith.

Of course, since the verses themselves interpret "the pass" here, the meaning of this interpretation must be that the primary pass is the "pass of the carnal soul," while freeing slaves and feeding the poor are clear examples of the struggle against it.

Other commentators have said that this "pass" refers to a difficult pass in the Hereafter, as mentioned in a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Indeed, before you is a steep pass which those burdened with heavy loads cannot cross, and I wish to lighten your burdens for that pass!" [1] Of course, this hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not intended as a direct commentary on this specific verse, but commentators have drawn this inference from it. This inference does not seem appropriate given the explicit interpretation provided within the verses themselves, unless the meaning is that the difficult passes of the Hereafter are the embodiment of the difficult and heavy acts of obedience in this world, and crossing those is contingent upon crossing these (reflect on this).

Notable here is the use of the word iqtahama from the root iqtiham, which originally means entering into a difficult and fearsome task (Mufradat al-Raghib) or entering and passing through something with intensity and hardship (Tafsir al-Kashshaf). This indicates that crossing this pass is not easy and reinforces what was stated at the beginning of the Surah:

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 495.

"We have certainly created man in hardship." Both his life is intertwined with suffering, and obedience to the Lord's command is accompanied by difficulties.

In a saying from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"Indeed, Paradise is surrounded by hardships, and the Fire is surrounded by desires." [1] Here, several points should be noted:

  1. The meaning of fakku raqabah is apparently the freeing of slaves and bondsmen.

In a hadith, it is narrated that a Bedouin man came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah! Teach me a deed that will enter me into Paradise." He said: "Though you spoke briefly, you have asked a great matter (or: though you spoke briefly, you expressed your purpose well)." Then He added: "Emancipate the soul and free the neck (from the yoke of slavery)."

The narrator asked: "Are these two not the same?"

He replied: "No, by the first I mean that you independently free a slave, and by the second that you assist in paying the price so they may be freed."

Then He added: "And return to the relative who has wronged you (and do good to them); if that is not possible, then feed the hungry, give drink to the thirsty, enjoin what is good, and forbid what is evil. If you cannot endure that, then restrain your tongue except from good." [1]

  1. Some commentators have interpreted fakku raqabah as freeing one's own neck from the burden of sins through repentance, or freeing oneself from divine punishment through enduring acts of obedience. However, given the verses that follow, which recommend care for the orphan and the needy, it apparently refers to the literal freeing of slaves.

  2. Masghabah is from the root saghab, meaning "hunger." Therefore, yawmin dhi masghabah means a day of hunger. Although the hungry have always existed in human societies, this expression emphasizes feeding the hungry during times of famine and drought to highlight the importance of this matter; otherwise, feeding the hungry has always been and remains among the most virtuous of deeds.

In a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever satiates a hungry person on a day of famine, Allah will admit him on the Day of Resurrection through one of the gates of Paradise through which no one else shall enter except those who have done the like of what he did." [2]

  1. Maqrabah means kinship and relationship. The emphasis on orphaned relatives is due to the consideration of priorities; otherwise, all orphans should be fed and comforted. This shows that relatives have a heavier responsibility regarding the orphans within their own family.

Furthermore, the abuses that occurred—especially in that era—regarding the property of orphaned relatives necessitated a specific warning about this difficult pass.

Abu al-Futuh Razi believes that maqrabah is not from the root of "kinship" but from "closeness," referring to orphans whose sides have seemingly stuck together due to the intensity of hunger. [1] However, this interpretation seems very unlikely.

  1. Matrabah is a mim-infinitive from the root tarab, originally derived from turab meaning "dust." It refers to someone who, due to extreme poverty, has become a "dweller of the dust." Again, the emphasis on such needy people is because of their priority; otherwise, feeding all the needy is a righteous deed.

In a hadith, it is narrated that whenever Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام wanted to eat, he would order a large tray to be placed near the table. He would take the best of every food on the table and place it in that tray, then order it to be taken to the needy. Then he would recite this verse: "But he has not attempted the steep pass..." and add: "Allah Almighty knew that not everyone is capable of freeing slaves, so He provided another path to His Paradise!" [2]

In the following verse, continuing the explanation of this difficult pass, It adds: "Then he was of those who believed and advised one another to patience and advised one another to compassion" (ثُمَّ كَانَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْمَرْحَمَةِ).

Thus, those who cross this difficult pass are those who possess faith, high morals such as calling to patience and human compassion, and have performed righteous deeds such as freeing slaves and feeding orphans and the needy.

Or, in other words, they step into three fields—faith, ethics, and action—and emerge successful and honored; these are the ones who can cross that difficult pass.

The use of thumma (then/after) does not always imply a chronological delay, which would suggest they first feed and spend and then believe. Rather, in such cases—as many commentators have specified—it is to express a superiority in rank, because the rank of faith and advising to patience and mercy is certainly higher than helping the needy. In fact, righteous deeds spring from that faith and morality, and the root of all of them must be sought in high beliefs and character.

Some have also suggested that thumma here implies a chronological delay, because good deeds sometimes become the source of an inclination toward faith and are especially effective in strengthening the foundations of morality, as human character first appears as an "act," then as a "state," then a "habit," and finally becomes a "firm trait" (malakah).

The expression tawasaw, which implies "exhorting one another," contains an important point: matters such as patience and steadfastness in the path of obedience to the Lord, struggling against the carnal soul, and strengthening the principle of love and mercy should not exist only individually in society. Rather, they must become a general current throughout the entire society, where all individuals advise one another to observe and preserve these "principles," thereby strengthening social bonds as well.

Some have said: "Patience" here refers to steadfastness in obeying Allah's command and attending to His orders, and "mercy" refers to love toward Allah's creation. We know that the foundation of religion consists of the connection with the "Creator" and the "creation." In any case, patience and steadfastness are the primary roots of every form of obedience, servitude, and the abandonment of sin and transgression.

At the end of these descriptions, It states the status of those who possess them: "Those are the companions of the right" (أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ).

Their record of deeds will be given to their right hand as a sign of acceptance in the presence of the Lord.

It is also possible that maymanah is from the root yumn and "blessing," meaning they are the possessors of blessing whose existence is a blessing for themselves and for society.

Then, turning to the opposite of this group—those who could not cross this difficult pass—It says: "But those who disbelieved in Our signs are the companions of the left" (وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِنَا هُمْ أَصْحَابُ الْمَشْأَمَةِ).

This is a sign that their hands are empty of good deeds and their record of deeds is blackened with sins.

Mash'amah is from the root shu'm (ill-omen), the opposite of maymanah from yumn. This means this group of disbelievers are ill-omened and unfortunate individuals who are the cause of their own misery and the misery of society. However, since being ill-omened or auspicious in the Hereafter is known by whether the record of deeds is in the left or right hand, some have accepted this interpretation, especially since the root shu'm in the language also carries the meaning of "inclining toward the left." [1]

In the final verse of this Surah, It makes a brief and meaningful reference to the punishment of the latter group, saying: "Over them will be a fire closed in" (عَلَيْهِمْ نَارٌ مُؤْصَدَةٌ), from which there is no escape.

Mu'sadah is from the root isad, meaning to close a door and secure it. It goes without saying that a person in a room where the air is slightly warm wants to open the doors so a breeze may blow and moderate the heat. Now, one must consider what state will occur in the blazing furnace of Hell when all the doors are closed?!

O Allah! Protect us from such a soul-melting punishment under the shadow of Your grace. O Lord! Crossing the passes that lie before us is not possible except by Your success; do not withhold Your success from us. O God! Place us in the ranks of the Companions of the Right, and gather us with the virtuous and the righteous.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Balad.
